title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a cultural resource management survey identifies a potentially significant archaeological site, the investigation does not stop there. Federal regulations and state historic preservation guidelines require a closer look before a project can move forward — and that closer look is called a Phase II archaeological evaluation. Understanding what this process involves helps project sponsors, federal agencies, and developers navigate CRM compliance efficiently and avoid costly surprises.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted excavation study that tests a site identified during Phase I survey to determine whether it meets the criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places. Archaeologists use systematic test units, artifact analysis, and site documentation to assess integrity, significance, and research potential.

Why Is a Phase II Evaluation Required?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that appears to have research potential or cultural significance. Federal law under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) requires agencies to assess whether their undertakings could affect historic properties — and a site's eligibility for the National Register is the threshold that matters.

Without a Phase II evaluation, there is no reliable way to determine whether a site's integrity is sufficient or whether it contains the kind of data that would make it significant under National Register Criterion D. Skipping this step can expose a project to regulatory delays, legal challenges, and costly last-minute design changes.

State historic preservation offices (SHPOs) and federal agencies such as the Army Corps of Engineers, FHWA, and USDA routinely require Phase II work before issuing permits or approvals for projects in sensitive areas. The evaluation provides the documented basis for a formal determination of eligibility (DOE) that satisfies Section 106 consultation requirements.

What Methods Are Used in a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation relies on systematic, controlled excavation designed to characterize a site's content, spatial extent, and stratigraphic integrity. The primary method is hand-excavated test units — typically 1×1 or 1×2 meter squares — placed in a statistically representative pattern across the site.

Archaeologists screen all excavated sediment through wire mesh (usually ¼-inch hardware cloth) to recover artifacts, ecofacts, and other cultural materials. Field technicians record soil descriptions, feature locations, and stratigraphic profiles for each unit. Specialized sampling — including flotation samples for botanical remains, bulk sediment samples for dating, and in some cases geomorphological coring — may be collected depending on site type and research questions.

Laboratory analysis follows fieldwork. Artifact processing, cataloging, and typological analysis provide information about the site's age, function, and cultural affiliation. Radiocarbon dating or other chronometric techniques may be employed to refine the site's temporal placement. The resulting data forms the evidentiary foundation for the eligibility recommendation submitted to the SHPO and lead federal agency.

GIS mapping and spatial analysis are increasingly standard components of Phase II work, allowing archaeologists to model feature distributions and site boundaries with greater precision. Learn more about how GIS and remote sensing enhance archaeological investigations.

How Is National Register Eligibility Determined?

National Register eligibility is evaluated against four criteria established by the National Park Service. Most archaeological sites are assessed under Criterion D, which recognizes properties that have yielded or may yield information important in prehistory or history. To qualify, a site must retain sufficient integrity and contain research potential that has not been exhausted.

Integrity refers to the degree to which a site retains its original physical character. Archaeologists assess seven aspects of integrity: location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association. For buried archaeological deposits, integrity of location and materials is often most critical — a site disturbed by deep plowing or construction grading may lack the stratigraphic context needed to yield reliable information.

The Phase II report presents a formal recommendation — eligible, not eligible, or needs data recovery — supported by artifact counts, feature descriptions, stratigraphic data, and comparative analysis. The SHPO and federal agency then consult to reach a consensus determination. If a site is found eligible and the project will adversely affect it, a Phase III data recovery program is typically required. Learn more about the full archaeological investigation process and how each phase builds on the last.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

The timeline for a Phase II archaeological evaluation varies based on site size, complexity, and report review cycles, but most projects follow a predictable sequence. Fieldwork typically spans one to three weeks for moderately sized sites, though larger or more complex sites may require more time in the field.

Laboratory analysis and report preparation generally take four to eight weeks after fieldwork concludes. The report is then submitted to the SHPO for a 30-day review period, during which the agency and SHPO reach a formal determination of eligibility. Complex sites or disagreements between parties can extend the review period.

From mobilization to final determination, project sponsors should budget approximately three to five months for the complete Phase II process under normal conditions. Early coordination with the lead federal agency and SHPO — and selecting a CRM firm with established state relationships — can help keep timelines on track. Understanding historic preservation compliance timelines early in project planning is essential for avoiding schedule disruptions.

What Does a Phase II Report Include?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation report is a formal technical document submitted to the SHPO and lead federal agency as part of Section 106 consultation. It must meet specific content requirements established by the SHPO and the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for Archaeological Documentation.

The report includes a project description and regulatory context, a review of previous research and background history, a detailed description of field methods, and a systematic presentation of results by excavation unit and stratigraphic level. Artifact tables, feature descriptions, photographs, maps, and stratigraphic profiles are standard components.

The analytical section synthesizes artifact and feature data within a regional research framework, situating the site's findings in relation to broader archaeological and historical questions. The report concludes with a clear eligibility recommendation and, if the site is found eligible, a discussion of the adverse effect determination and potential mitigation options. A well-written Phase II report is the cornerstone of a defensible Section 106 record.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that appears potentially eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. The lead federal agency, in consultation with the SHPO, determines whether Phase II testing is warranted based on the site's apparent research potential, integrity, and the project's area of potential effect.

Who pays for a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Under Section 106, the project proponent — typically the permit applicant, developer, or federal agency sponsoring the undertaking — is responsible for funding all phases of cultural resource investigation. This cost is treated as part of standard project compliance, similar to environmental impact assessments and engineering surveys.

Can a project proceed if a Phase II site is found eligible?

Yes, but additional steps are required. If a Phase II evaluation finds a site eligible and the project will adversely affect it, Section 106 requires the agency to consult with the SHPO and other consulting parties to resolve the adverse effect — typically through Phase III data recovery excavation, avoidance, or a programmatic agreement that defines mitigation measures.

What is the difference between Phase I, Phase II, and Phase III archaeology?

Phase I is a reconnaissance survey to identify and record archaeological sites within a project area. Phase II evaluates identified sites to determine National Register eligibility through targeted testing. Phase III is large-scale data recovery excavation conducted when an eligible site cannot be avoided and must be mitigated prior to project construction.

Partner with Experienced CRM Archaeologists

Navigating the Phase II archaeological evaluation process requires expertise in field methodology, regulatory compliance, and SHPO coordination. A thorough, well-documented evaluation protects your project schedule and ensures defensible results that hold up to agency review. Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) brings more than 14 years of experience conducting Phase II evaluations for federal agencies, state DOTs, and private developers across the Southeast.

Our RPA-certified archaeologists understand the nuances of Section 106 consultation and work proactively with SHPOs and lead agencies to keep your project moving. excerpt: A Phase II archaeological evaluation determines if a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places through targeted excavation and artifact analysis.
